ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers lamb and mutton meat dishes; no specific pepper masala preparation code exists. Broader category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for mutton pepper masala preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Mutton Pepper Masala is approximately 200 g, representing one medium-sized restaurant or home portion consisting of 3–4 pieces of mutton with masala gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Pepper Masala — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Pepper Masala (cr_id G00010723). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat curry with low-GI spice base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Pepper Masala. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 7.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g; the carbohydrate fraction derives from onion, tomato, and spices — all low-GI ingredients. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (14.88 g from 200 g portion) / 100 = 5.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg per 100 g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 934 mg sodium, representing 41% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as papadum, pickles, or salted rice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44 g per 100 g), low sugar (4.69 g per 100 g), high protein (14.05 g per 100 g), and estimated low GI (~35) support glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Pepper Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (100–150 g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the high protein content supports satiety and blunts postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 85 mg per 100 g and total fat at 11.58 g per 100 g; saturated fat breakdown not available from HPB source but red meat masala preparations typically carry 4–5 g saturated fat per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100 g), request reduced-oil preparation, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to offset the cholesterol and saturated fat load from mutton.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit red meat consumption; the anti-inflammatory piperine in black pepper provides insufficient offset against the arachidonic acid load from mutton.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Pepper Masala is a robust South Indian and Singapore Indian dry-style curry dish made from bone-in or boneless mutton (goat or lamb) cooked with freshly cracked black pepper, onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, and whole spices including cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom. The dish is prepared by sautéing the aromatics in oil, adding the mutton and spices, and cooking until the meat is tender and coated in a thick, fragrant masala. The flavour profile is intensely peppery, savoury, and mildly spiced, with a dry or semi-dry texture. It is a popular lunch and dinner dish in Singapore's Indian hawker stalls and restaurants, particularly among the Tamil and South Indian communities. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, 7.44 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The sodium level is elevated and warrants caution for hypertensive individuals. The low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycemic index (~35) make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion-controlled. The cholesterol content (85 mg per 100 g) and red meat base require moderation for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. Black pepper's active compound piperine has documented anti-inflammatory and bioavailability-enhancing properties.

How much sodium is in mutton pepper masala?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with an exact match for Mutton Pepper Masala (cr_id G00010723) at confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present from the primary approved source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Mutton Pepper Masala is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB source for this dish entry. GI is an analogous estimate (~35, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed meat curry preparations; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Pepper Masala exists in the approved GI database. Halal status assessed as likely halal given the dish's prevalence in Singapore's Muslim-majority Indian hawker context, but individual preparation should be verified. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
